nodes.label: P10_T001
nodes.identity_summary: This consumer is a strategic, price-value focused student with low brand loyalty who prioritizes fragrance (especially oceanic/musky scents) and tangible skin benefits. Their purchase decisions are driven by online reviews and ratings from the purchase platform itself, with a strong preference for dermatologist or clinical validation over influencer hype. They balance mood-dependent grooming with a pragmatic approach, willing to experiment but only after thorough research and when the price fits their established budget of ₹200-₹350.

nodes.label: Balanced and Context-Dependent
nodes.dimension: Visual versus textual information processing preference

nodes.label: Cautious Evaluator
nodes.dimension: Impulse versus deliberate purchase decision speed

nodes.label: strategic-value-maximizer
nodes.description: Carefully weighs price against quality, seeking optimal value rather than cheapest or premium options, and plans purchases based on running out rather than impulse.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Q: What is the maximum premium (%) you would pay for a product that aligns with your values? Rate each separately: A: {"Clinical-grade, lab-tested formulation":"10_20", "Made in India":"10_20"}

[SYNTHETIC] Q: You see a body wash at ₹349 that claims '100% recyclable packaging' and donates ₹10 per bottle to ocean cleanup. A nearly identical body wash without these claims costs ₹269. How much of the ₹80 difference do you feel is justified? A: ₹0 — these claims don't influence what I'm willing to pay.

[BRANCH] Q: A body wash brand you've never tried sends you a ₹150 discount coupon valid for 48 hours... A: I'd let the coupon expire. My current body wash works fine, and I prefer to stick with what I know until I actually run out.
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a 'premium' body wash with sustainability claims but a ₹80+ price hike, this person would likely dismiss it and choose a cheaper, functionally equivalent alternative.

nodes.label: fragrance-driven-with-oceanic-musky-preference
nodes.description: Consistently ranks fragrance as the #1 purchase factor, specifically gravitating towards ocean/aquatic and woody/musky scents, and cites smell as the primary reason for satisfaction with a product.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Q: When buying any personal care product, rank these factors from most to least important: A: ["fragrance","price","ingredients","reviews","packaging","brand","recommendation","expert"]
[REAL] Q: What fragrance families do you naturally gravitate toward? Pick your top 2: A: Ocean_aquatic,woody_musky
[REAL] Q: What was it about the new body wash that made you feel satisfied after trying it? A: Smell
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a new body wash concept with a compelling oceanic or woody fragrance story front-and-center, this person would be highly likely to stop and engage, regardless of brand.

nodes.label: platform-review-reliant
nodes.description: Heavily trusts and relies on aggregated star ratings and review counts on the e-commerce platform (Amazon, Nykaa) where they are shopping at that moment, valuing volume of data over influencer or social media opinions.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Q: When shopping online for personal care, what do you look at first? Pick your top 3: A: ["review_count","price","star_rating"]
[REAL] Q: Are there specific review platforms or trusted sources you rely on for these ratings when deciding on a new product? A: Relies on ratings from the purchase platform itself

[SYNTHETIC] Q: You're browsing Nykaa and find two products at ₹349. One has 2,400 ratings with a 4.1 average. The other has 85 ratings with a 4.8 average. Which one do you click on first? A: I'd click on the one with 2,400 ratings and 4.1 average first.
nodes.predictive_value: When a product page lacks a substantial number of reviews (e.g., <500), this person would likely hesitate and move to a more reviewed alternative, even if the new product has a higher average rating.

nodes.label: clinical-expert-validation-seeker
nodes.description: Actively seeks and places high trust in recommendations from dermatologists, clinical testing, and scientific claims, ranking them above social proof and influencer endorsements.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Q: What claims or product descriptions on a body wash would make you stop and read more? Pick top 3: A: ["india_specific","derm_recommended","ph_balanced"]
[BRANCH] Q: Rank these five purchase triggers from most to least likely... A: 1) A dermatologist's recommendation in a YouTube video – expert validation is crucial for skin care decisions.
[SYNTHETIC] Q: You have 10 'trust points' to allocate across these sources... A: E) Dermatologist's Instagram post - 4, D) Amazon India reviews - 3...
nodes.predictive_value: When shown an ad or product page that prominently features a 'Dermatologist Recommended' badge or clinical study results, this person's attention and purchase intent would increase significantly.

nodes.label: low-brand-loyalty-experimenter
nodes.description: Explicitly states low loyalty to personal care brands, enjoys trying new options when the need arises, and the last switch was triggered simply by their old product being out of stock.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Q: How loyal are you to personal care brands? A: Low_loyalty

[REAL] Q: Describe the last time you tried a completely new body wash brand. What triggered the switch? Were you satisfied? A: Last tried a new brand because my old one was out of stock. Was satisfied.
[SYNTHETIC] Q: Think about the last 3 body washes you purchased. Were they: A: C) Different brands each time.
nodes.predictive_value: When a competitor brand offers a compelling discount or a new variant that aligns with their preferences, this person would switch without hesitation, feeling no attachment to their current brand.

nodes.label: strategic-browser-not-impulse-buyer
nodes.description: Does not buy personal care products until the current one is nearly empty, then browses online with intent, resisting time-limited deals if not in active need.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Q: When your current body wash/soap runs out, what is your typical next step? A: Browse_online

[BRANCH] Q: A body wash brand you've never tried sends you a ₹150 discount coupon valid for 48 hours... A: I'd let the coupon expire. My current body wash works fine, and I prefer to stick with what I know until I actually run out.

[SYNTHETIC] Q: You're browsing Nykaa and find your regular body wash at ₹349. There's a combo offer: buy 2 for ₹549... A: Buy just one at ₹349. I don’t like stocking up unnecessarily, especially when I only need one now.
nodes.predictive_value: Flash sales or limited-time coupons sent when they are not in immediate need of a product will be ignored, as their purchase cycle is strictly need-based.

nodes.label: price-anchor-200-350-inr
nodes.description: Has a well-defined and consistent price comfort zone for body wash (₹200-₹350), uses this as a primary filter, and is reluctant to exceed it without clear, proven justification.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Q: How much do you typically spend on a single body wash purchase? A: 200_350

[SYNTHETIC] Q: A friend recommends a body wash they love, but it costs ₹599 for 200ml — roughly double what you usually spend... A: Stick with current body wash. Liking it but not enough to pay double the price.

[SYNTHETIC] Q: You're buying body wash on Zepto and notice your usual brand now shows 'Price increased: was ₹249, now ₹299.' A comparable alternative brand is still at ₹249. What's your most likely reaction? A: Switch to ₹249 alternative.
nodes.predictive_value: A new body wash priced at ₹399+ will be immediately scrutinized for extraordinary benefits; if perceived as only incrementally better, they will reject it in favor of options within their anchor range.

nodes.label: mood-aware-but-not-mood-driven-buyer
nodes.description: Acknowledges that mood influences their grooming routine and attention to ads, but actively prevents transient emotions from overriding their core rational decision criteria (price, reviews, ingredients).
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Q: How would you describe your relationship with bathing/grooming? A: Mood_dependent

[BRANCH] Q: You've had a long, stressful day. While scrolling through social media, you see an ad for a new body wash with a calming lavender scent... A: My mood might make me notice the ad more when stressed, but I wouldn't click immediately—I'd save it or look up reviews later when I'm calmer. The decision ultimately depends on the product's features...

[SYNTHETIC] Q: During a video call with friends, someone notices your body wash... and makes a joke about it being a 'basic' brand. How do you react? A: Laugh it off — I don't care what they think about my body wash.
nodes.predictive_value: Emotional or 'self-care' themed ads might capture their attention during a bad day, but will not lead to a purchase unless the product also meets their rational criteria for value and efficacy.

nodes.label: ingredient-skeptic-not-dogmatist
nodes.description: Pays attention to specific ingredient claims (sulfate-free, pH-balanced) and will verify labels, but is not ideologically committed to 'natural' and is skeptical of marketing buzzwords without scientific backing.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Q: Which certifications or labels influence your purchase? Select all that apply. A: Ph_balanced,sulfate_free
[SYNTHETIC] Q: You notice your body wash has 'Paraben-Free' printed on it. What would you most likely do? A: B) Flip the bottle to verify in the ingredient list.
[SYNTHETIC] Q: If a body wash product on Nykaa had these tabs, in what order would you click through them? Rank 1-5: A: Customer Reviews, Ingredients, Product Description...
nodes.predictive_value: When a product claims 'clean' or 'natural,' this person would first check for the absence of specific ingredients they avoid (like sulfates) rather than being swayed by the holistic claim itself.

nodes.label: quick-commerce-for-substitutes-not-discovery
nodes.description: Uses quick commerce (Blinkit, Zepto) for urgent replenishment and is willing to accept a close substitute from the limited selection, but prefers other channels for initial research and discovery.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Q: Do you use quick commerce (Blinkit, Zepto, Swiggy Instamart) for personal care? If yes, does the limited selection change what you buy? A: Yes; would buy a close substitute from limited selection

[SYNTHETIC] Q: You're in a rush and ordering body wash on Zepto/Blinkit for 10-minute delivery. Your usual brand isn't available... How do you choose? A: D) Pick the one closest to your usual brand's description (e.g., moisturizing, gel-based).
[SYNTHETIC] Q: You have 10 minutes before leaving for work and realize you're out of body wash. Rank these actions... A: (b) Use a bar soap as backup, (a) Order on Zepto for 10-min delivery...
nodes.predictive_value: For a new brand to succeed with this person on quick commerce, it must be positioned as a clear, logical substitute to a mainstream brand (e.g., 'Like Dove, but with X').

nodes.label: self-aware-scent-chooser
nodes.description: Chooses fragrances primarily for personal enjoyment ('one that I love') while also caring about how they smell to others, indicating a balanced, self-aware approach to scent selection.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Q: How much do you care about how you smell to others throughout the day? Is grooming more about feeling good yourself, or about how others perceive you, or both? A: Both
[REAL] Q: When you're getting ready for the day, do you find yourself more focused on choosing a scent that you love personally, or one that you think others will find appealing? A: One that I love
[SYNTHETIC] Q: You're in a long-distance relationship and your partner mentions they love a specific body wash scent. Would you: A: B) Appreciate the input but stick with what I like.
nodes.predictive_value: Ads that focus solely on 'being attractive to others' will be less effective than those that balance personal sensory pleasure with subtle social confidence.

nodes.label: problem-aware-skin-benefit-seeker
nodes.description: Notices skin problems (dryness, rough texture) and actively seeks products that offer tangible, described benefits like moisturization and improved skin appearance, beyond just cleansing.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Q: How much attention do you pay to how your skin looks and feels on a daily basis? A: Notice_problems
[REAL] Q: Could you share what specific benefits you would want from this body wash, beyond the packaging and price? A: Should moisturize dry skin and improve skin appearance
[SYNTHETIC] Q: Allocate 100 points across these five factors based on what actually drives your final body wash purchase decision... A: Price/deal: 40, Past experience: 30, Skin benefit: 20...
nodes.predictive_value: Product concepts that clearly articulate how they solve specific, common skin issues (e.g., '24-hour moisturization for dry, rough skin') will resonate more than generic 'feel clean' messages.

nodes.label: skeptical-of-social-media-hype
nodes.description: Distrusts social media influencers and paid reviews, and has never purchased a personal care product based solely on social media promotion.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Q: Have you ever bought a personal care product because you saw it on social media? Describe what happened. A: No
[REAL] Q: Is there something specific about social media platforms that makes you hesitant to trust them for product recommendations? A: Paid reviews

[SYNTHETIC] Q: Two influencers you follow reviewing body washes on the same day... Who influences your purchase more? A: Influencer A, the skincare expert. Expert opinion matters more than follower count.
nodes.predictive_value: Influencer-led launch campaigns or ads tagged '#sponsored' will be met with skepticism and are unlikely to drive a direct purchase without additional validation from expert or crowd-sourced reviews.

nodes.label: format-preference-thick-cream-granular
nodes.description: Has a specific, sensory-driven preference for body wash texture: a thick, creamy liquid with a slightly granular feel that provides a cooling sensation and leaves no stickiness.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Q: What texture do you prefer in a body wash? A: Thick_cream

[REAL] Q: Is there anything about the texture or how it feels on your skin that you wish could be improved? A: Three key things: (1) granular texture in the liquid, (2) cooling sensation during use, (3) no stickiness left on skin after wash
[REAL] Q: If you could design your PERFECT body wash from scratch, describe it... A: Texture: slightly granular, thick liquid.
nodes.predictive_value: Product demonstrations or samples that allow them to physically feel a thick, granular texture will be far more persuasive than visual ads alone.

nodes.label: indian-value-perception-bias
nodes.description: Believes Indian brands (or brands perceived as offering value in India, like Dove) offer better quality for price compared to international ones, but is not patriotic; it's a pragmatic value assessment.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Q: When considering price and quality, do you find that Indian or international brands tend to offer better value for you personally? A: Indian
[REAL] Q: Could you share an example of an Indian brand that you feel delivers great quality for its price compared to an international one? A: Dove

[SYNTHETIC] Q: A body wash brand offers two identical products with different labels: one says 'Made in India' priced at ₹249, the other says 'Imported from Korea' priced at ₹449... A: Buy the 'Made in India' at ₹249. Price and origin matter more than imported label.
nodes.predictive_value: Messaging that emphasizes 'Great quality for the Indian price point' or 'Formulated for value in India' will align with their existing belief and increase perceived appropriateness.

nodes.label: substitution-tolerant-in-urgency
nodes.description: When faced with stock-outs or urgent need, demonstrates high tolerance for trying unfamiliar brands that appear similar in description or type to their usual product.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Q: Do you use quick commerce (Blinkit, Zepto, Swiggy Instamart) for personal care? If yes, does the limited selection change what you buy? A: Yes; would buy a close substitute from limited selection

[SYNTHETIC] Q: You're at a Reliance Smart Bazaar and notice your regular body wash is out of stock... Which do you pick? Rank them 1-3 in order of preference. A: 1) Popular Indian brand I've seen ads for, 2) Well-known international brand, 3) Store-brand option.
[SYNTHETIC] Q: You're traveling for a week and forgot your body wash... What do you buy? A: Buy the local store's unfamiliar brand for ₹99.
nodes.predictive_value: In out-of-stock scenarios on e-commerce or in stores, this person is a prime target for 'alternate product' recommendations or shelf placement next to market leaders.

nodes.label: deal-evaluator-clear-savings-over-bundles
nodes.description: Prefers straightforward discounts (e.g., '₹100 off') over complex bundling ('Buy 2 Get 1 Free') or future credits, valuing immediate, transparent price reduction.
evidence_quotes: [SYNTHETIC] Q: Rank these discount presentations from most to least appealing: A: A) '₹100 off — now ₹199', B) '33% off', E) 'Free shipping (worth ₹50) + ₹50 off', C) 'Buy 2 Get 1 Free', D) '₹100 cashback on Paytm'

[SYNTHETIC] Q: Two body wash brands you equally like are running Diwali offers... Brand A offers buy-2-get-1-free. Brand B offers a ₹100 cashback on a single bottle. You only need one bottle right now. Which offer do you pick and why? A: Choose the ₹100 cashback on a single bottle since I only need one now.

[SYNTHETIC] Q: If you had to choose between these two deals for body wash, which would you pick? Deal A: Buy 1 body wash (250ml) at ₹299 and get a free loofah worth ₹99. Deal B: Buy 1 body wash (250ml) at ₹259, no freebies. A: Deal B: Buy 1 body wash (250ml) at ₹259, no freebies.
nodes.predictive_value: Promotions should lead with a simple, immediate price cut. BOGO or gift-with-purchase offers will be less effective unless the net price per unit is clearly and obviously the lowest.

nodes.label: discovery-through-traditional-and-in-store
nodes.description: Discovers new products primarily through TV ads, in-store browsing, and quick commerce apps, blending traditional and modern retail touchpoints.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Q: Where do you typically discover new personal care products? Pick your top 3: A: ["tv","in_store","quick_commerce"]

[SYNTHETIC] Q: You discover a new body wash through three different sources on the same day... Rank these three in order of how likely each would be to make you actually purchase the product. A: Sample at Shoppers Stop, friend's Instagram story, sponsored Amazon result.
[BRANCH] Q: Two body wash ads play on TV... A: Neither ad alone would convince me; I'd need to see more about the product's specifics... The ad style is secondary...
nodes.predictive_value: Integrated campaigns that use TV to build awareness, coupled with strong in-store visibility and quick commerce availability, will effectively reach this person across their discovery journey.

nodes.label: research-before-commitment-on-new-brands
nodes.description: For new or unfamiliar brands, requires a low-risk trial option (mini/travel size, sachet, sample) or extensive review validation before committing to a full-size purchase.
evidence_quotes: [SYNTHETIC] Q: A body wash brand offers two purchase options: (A) Full-size 250ml bottle for ₹349 with no returns, (B) A 50ml trial for ₹99... Which option do you choose? A: Choose the 50ml trial for ₹99 with credit toward the full size.
[SYNTHETIC] Q: A new D2C body wash brand launches in India... at what stage would you consider buying? A: After it gets listed on Amazon/Nykaa with 100+ reviews.
[SYNTHETIC] Q: You had a bad experience with a new body wash last month... Do you: A: B) Only try if they offer a 50ml trial size.
nodes.predictive_value: New D2C brands must offer affordable trial sizes or have robust review portfolios on mainstream platforms to convert this person; a direct full-price ask will fail.

nodes.label: pragmatic-sustainability-indifferent
nodes.description: Explicitly assigns zero premium value to sustainability, eco-friendly packaging, or charitable causes; product performance and price dominate the decision.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Q: What is the maximum premium (%) you would pay for a product that aligns with your values? Rate each separately: A: {"Sustainable/eco-friendly packaging":"0", "Ayurvedic or traditional ingredients":"0", "Cruelty-free, not tested on animals":"0"}

[SYNTHETIC] Q: You see a body wash at ₹349 that claims '100% recyclable packaging' and donates ₹10 per bottle to ocean cleanup... How much of the ₹80 difference do you feel is justified? A: C) ₹0 — these claims don't influence what I'm willing to pay.
[SYNTHETIC] Q: A body wash brand runs a 'transparency challenge' — they publish their full cost breakdown... Does this: A: Make me compare this breakdown with other brands.
nodes.predictive_value: Lead with fragrance, skin benefit, and price in messaging; sustainability claims should be relegated to the background as they provide no incentive.

nodes.label: packaging-agnostic-function-over-form
nodes.description: States they 'don't care' about packaging and prefers a natural look, indicating that bottle aesthetics are a negligible factor as long as the product inside meets functional needs.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Q: If you could design your PERFECT body wash from scratch, describe it. Think about fragrance, texture, benefit, packaging, price — anything. A: ...Packaging: don't care.
[REAL] Q: What is your ideal post-wash skin feeling? A: Moisturized

[SYNTHETIC] Q: Imagine two body washes are identical in ingredients and fragrance. One is packaged in a sleek, minimalist bottle and priced at ₹399. The other is in a basic plastic bottle priced at ₹249. Both are 250ml. Which do you buy? A: Buy the basic plastic bottle at ₹249. Packaging doesn’t justify ₹150 price difference.
nodes.predictive_value: Investing in ultra-premium packaging will not command a price premium from this person; resources are better spent on product formulation and clear benefit communication.

nodes.label: P10_T002
nodes.identity_summary: This person is a pragmatic, low-loyalty student who prioritizes fragrance and skin benefits over brand names. Their purchases are driven by a careful balance of price sensitivity and proven efficacy, with strong reliance on platform reviews and a preference for Indian-made products offering better value. While mood influences attention to marketing, they're ultimately a strategic decision-maker who distrusts hype and values personal experience and trusted recommendations over social media influence.

nodes.label: Visual-Aesthetic Enthusiast
nodes.dimension: Visual versus textual information processing preference

nodes.label: Selective Benefit Seeker
nodes.dimension: Impulse versus deliberate purchase decision speed

nodes.label: fragrance-prime-decision-driver
nodes.description: Chooses body wash primarily based on fragrance, ranking it as the most important factor and citing smell as the key satisfaction driver in past trials.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] A: ["fragrance","price","ingredients","reviews","packaging","brand","recommendation","expert"]
[REAL] Q: What was it about the new body wash that made you feel satisfied after trying it? A: Smell
[REAL] Q: How would you categorize body washes in your mind? What makes one different from another? A: Primarily differentiated by smell
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a new body wash concept, this person would first evaluate its fragrance description and likely ignore products that don't highlight scent prominently.

nodes.label: price-value-balancer-not-extremist
nodes.description: Carefully weighs price against quality and size, willing to pay up to ₹350 normally and occasionally more for better value-per-ml, but switches brands readily to maintain spending levels when prices increase.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Q: How much do you typically spend on a single body wash purchase? A: 200_350
[SYNTHETIC] Q: What if your regular body wash suddenly became available only in a 500ml size at ₹549 (previously 250ml at ₹299)? A: Buy the larger size — better value per ml
[SYNTHETIC] Q: What if body wash prices across all brands increased by 20% overnight? A: Switch to a cheaper brand to maintain my old spending level
nodes.predictive_value: When presented with a price increase, this person would likely switch to a cheaper alternative rather than absorb the cost, unless the product offers exceptional value per ml.

nodes.label: platform-review-dependent
nodes.description: Heavily relies on review counts and star ratings from the purchase platform itself (Amazon, Nykaa) when making decisions, trusting volume of reviews over expert opinions.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Q: When shopping online for personal care, what do you look at first? Pick your top 3: A: ["review_count","price","star_rating"]
[REAL] Q: Are there specific review platforms or trusted sources you rely on for these ratings when deciding on a new product? A: Relies on ratings from the purchase platform itself

[SYNTHETIC] Q: You're comparing body washes online and see these ratings: Brand A: 4.2 stars, 15,000 reviews, ₹279. Brand B: 4.8 stars, 120 reviews, ₹329. Which do you trust more and buy? A: Brand A: 4.2 stars, 15,000 reviews, ₹279
nodes.predictive_value: A new product with few reviews would struggle to gain their attention unless accompanied by a compelling trial offer or personal recommendation.

nodes.label: personal-connection-trumps-digital-hype
nodes.description: Values recommendations from friends seen in real-life settings far more than influencer endorsements or digital advertising.
evidence_quotes: [BRANCH] Q: Rank these five purchase triggers from most to least likely... A: I'd go: 1) Seeing it in my best friend's bathroom – personal connections make me curious and trusting.

[SYNTHETIC] Q: You see a body wash ad on Instagram featuring a dermatologist... vs. your close friend casually mentions they love a completely different body wash. A: Buy my close friend's recommended body wash
[REAL] Q: Who do you follow or trust in the personal care space on social media? A: None
nodes.predictive_value: Marketing that leverages peer-to-peer sharing or real-world placement in friends' homes would be more effective than celebrity or influencer campaigns.

nodes.label: low-brand-loyalty-experimenter
nodes.description: Explicitly states low loyalty to personal care brands, switches frequently based on immediate needs, and has purchased different brands in recent history.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Q: How loyal are you to personal care brands? A: Low_loyalty
[REAL] Q: Describe the last time you tried a completely new body wash brand. What triggered the switch? A: Last tried a new brand because my old one was out of stock.
[SYNTHETIC] Q: Think about the last 3 body washes you purchased. Were they: A: C) Different brands each time.
nodes.predictive_value: This person is easily convertible from competitors with a compelling offer or feature that meets their core needs (fragrance, price, skin benefit).

nodes.label: strategic-browser-not-impulse-buyer
nodes.description: When current product runs out, browses online rather than automatically repurchasing, indicating a deliberate, information-seeking approach to each purchase cycle.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Q: When your current body wash/soap runs out, what is your typical next step? A: Browse_online

[BRANCH] Q: A body wash brand you've never tried sends you a ₹150 discount coupon... Your current body wash still has about 2 weeks of use left. A: I'd browse the website first to check if the body wash aligns with my preferences.
[SYNTHETIC] Q: During a Nykaa Pink Friday sale, you have 5 body washes in your cart. You only have time to research 2... A: The brands I've never tried to discover new options worth exploring.
nodes.predictive_value: Timing marketing messages for when they're likely browsing (post-run-out) would be more effective than random interruption advertising.

nodes.label: mood-modulates-marketing-receptivity
nodes.description: Own grooming routine and responsiveness to certain product claims (like stress relief) vary significantly with current mood states.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Q: How would you describe your relationship with bathing/grooming? A: Mood_dependent

[BRANCH] Q: You've had a long, stressful day. While scrolling through social media, you see an ad for a new body wash with a calming lavender scent... A: After a stressful day, I'm much more likely to click on that ad and consider buying the body wash for instant relief
nodes.predictive_value: Contextual ads tied to stress-relief or mood enhancement would have higher engagement during evening or weekend scrolling versus morning routines.

nodes.label: ingredient-skeptic-requires-proof
nodes.description: Questions the efficacy of novel ingredients in rinse-off formats and requires clinical evidence or detailed explanations rather than accepting marketing claims at face value.
evidence_quotes: [SYNTHETIC] Q: A new body wash uses 'bakuchiol' — a plant-based retinol alternative... What's your primary hesitation? A: Body wash washes off — would the ingredient even work?
[SYNTHETIC] Q: What if your regular ₹299 body wash launched a 'dermatologist-recommended' version at ₹399 with the same ingredients... A: I'd need to see the clinical study results first
[REAL] Q: What claims or product descriptions on a body wash would make you stop and read more? A: ["india_specific","derm_recommended","ph_balanced"]
nodes.predictive_value: Innovative ingredient stories must include clear explanations of mechanism of action in a wash-off format to overcome skepticism.

nodes.label: india-specific-formulation-seeker
nodes.description: Strongly interested in products formulated for Indian skin types and hard water conditions, viewing this as a relevant and meaningful benefit.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Q: Would you be interested in a body wash specifically formulated for Indian skin types and hard water conditions? A: {"rating":5,"explanation":""}
[REAL] Q: What claims or product descriptions on a body wash would make you stop and read more? A: ["india_specific","derm_recommended","ph_balanced"]

[SYNTHETIC] Q: You see a body wash on Amazon India with the description: 'Made with an ancient tribal recipe from Northeast India...' A: Would try it; primary driver is the unique India-specific tribal recipe
nodes.predictive_value: Product concepts highlighting India-specific formulation or adaptation to local conditions would capture immediate attention and consideration.

nodes.label: pragmatic-packaging-preference
nodes.description: Prefers natural-looking packaging aesthetically but won't pay extra for it; values functionality and price over design premium.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Q: Do you prefer packaging that looks: A: Natural

[SYNTHETIC] Q: Imagine two body washes are identical in ingredients and fragrance. One is packaged in a sleek, minimalist bottle and priced at ₹399. The other is in a basic plastic bottle priced at ₹249. A: Buy the ₹249 basic plastic bottle since packaging doesn't justify the price difference
[SYNTHETIC] Q: Your body wash brand collaborates with a popular Indian fashion designer for a limited-edition packaging... A: Skip it — I'm paying for the product, not the bottle.
nodes.predictive_value: Premium packaging without corresponding formula benefits would be dismissed as a marketing gimmick not worth paying for.

nodes.label: quick-commerce-substitute-acceptor
nodes.description: Uses quick commerce for personal care purchases and is willing to accept a close substitute from the limited selection when needed urgently.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Q: Do you use quick commerce (Blinkit, Zepto, Swiggy Instamart) for personal care? If yes, does the limited selection change what you buy? A: Yes; would buy a close substitute from limited selection

[REAL] Q: If you could design your PERFECT body wash from scratch, describe it. Think about fragrance... A: Fragrance: ocean.

nodes.label: texture-preference-thick-granular

nodes.label: trusts-indian-value-proposition

nodes.label: certification-selective-ph-sulfate-free

nodes.label: strategic-deal-jumper

[BRANCH] Q64: A body wash brand you've never tried sends you a ₹150 discount coupon... Do you: A) Use the coupon immediately. As a strategic shopper, ₹249 for a ₹399 body wash is a smart save, and stocking up now makes sense for future use.

[SYNTHETIC] Q67: You're browsing Nykaa and find your regular body wash at ₹349. There's a combo offer: buy 2 for ₹549 (saving ₹149)... A: Buy the combo to save ₹149. Strategic to stock up when there's a clear price advantage.
nodes.predictive_value: Flash sales, limited-time coupons, and clear bundle savings are highly effective at triggering an immediate purchase, even for unfamiliar brands, because they appeal to their strategic, value-calculating mindset.

nodes.label: mood-aware-but-not-driven

[BRANCH] Q65: You've had a long, stressful day. While scrolling through social media, you see an ad for a new body wash with a calming lavender scent and claims of stress relief... A: My mood might make me notice the ad more when stressed, but I wouldn't click immediately—I'd save it or look up reviews later when I'm calmer. The decision ultimately depends on the product's features... I'm aware of my moods but try not to let them override my usual criteria.
nodes.predictive_value: Emotional or mood-based advertising (e.g., 'self-care ritual', 'energy boost') might increase top-of-mind awareness but will not close the sale unless paired with strong functional claims (dermatologist-recommended, pH-balanced).

nodes.label: platform-rating-reliant

nodes.label: quick-commerce-substitute-buyer
