[REAL] How important is it to you that a product is 'natural' or 'chemical-free'? And what does 'natural' mean to you in your own words? A: {"rating":3,"explanation":"Made from majority of natural products"}
nodes.predictive_value: When comparing two body washes, one 'Made in India with clinical testing' at ₹330 and an imported one at ₹300, they'd choose the former. A '100% natural, eco-friendly' claim at a premium would be ignored.

nodes.label: ingredient-list-skeptic-but-key-component-checker
nodes.description: Does not deeply analyze full ingredient lists but scans for specific, recognized markers of quality or concern (e.g., pH-balanced, sulfate-free) and verifies claims against the list.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Which certifications or labels influence your purchase? Select all that apply. A: Ph_balanced,sulfate_free
[SYNTHETIC] Flip the bottle to verify in the ingredient list.
[SYNTHETIC] Recognize a few key ones like SLS.
[SYNTHETIC] Choose the detailed ingredient breakdown with percentages.
nodes.predictive_value: If a product claims 'Sulfate-Free', this person would quickly glance at the ingredient list to confirm, but wouldn't spend time researching every chemical component.

nodes.label: mood-independent-rational-evaluator
nodes.description: Purchase decisions and responses to marketing are deliberately insulated from transient emotional states; they maintain a consistent, rational evaluation framework based on product merits.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] How would you describe your relationship with bathing/grooming? A: Mood_dependent
[BRANCH] My mood doesn't really change how I respond to ads—even after a long day, I'd evaluate the body wash based on its claims, price, and if it fits my needs...
[SYNTHETIC] Use a special/premium body wash you reserve for important occasions.
nodes.predictive_value: An ad using emotional, stress-relief messaging would be less effective; this person would respond better to an ad listing clinical benefits, price, and review ratings.

nodes.label: indian-brand-value-perception-lean
nodes.description: Perceives Indian brands as offering better value for money compared to international ones, though holds no strong bias and will choose based on the final price-quality equation.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] When considering price and quality, do you find that Indian or international brands tend to offer better value for you personally? A: Indian
[REAL] Could you share an example of an Indian brand that you feel delivers great quality for its price compared to an international one? A: Dove
[SYNTHETIC] Buy 'Made in India' at ₹249; price and origin align better with my values and budget.
nodes.predictive_value: When presented with a new Indian D2C brand and a familiar international brand at the same price point, they'd be more inclined to try the Indian brand, expecting better value.

nodes.label: deal-mechanism-preference-for-transparent-cash-discount
nodes.description: Prefers straightforward, immediate price reductions (e.g., '₹100 off') over complex bundling ('Buy 2 Get 1 Free') or future-value promotions (cashback), which are seen as less trustworthy or inconvenient.
evidence_quotes: [SYNTHETIC] Deal B: ₹259 with no freebies. I prefer straightforward savings over free loofahs.

[SYNTHETIC] Rank these discount presentations from most to least appealing: A: '₹100 off — now ₹199' B: '33% off' C: 'Buy 2 Get 1 Free' D: '₹100 cashback on Paytm' E: 'Free shipping (worth ₹50) + ₹50 off' A: A) ₹100 off — now ₹199, B) '33% off', E) 'Free shipping + ₹50 off', C) 'Buy 2 Get 1 Free', D) '₹100 cashback on Paytm'.
[SYNTHETIC] Amazon listing with 'MRP ₹799, 25% off' feels like a better deal despite same final price.
nodes.predictive_value: A marketing email with the subject 'FLAT ₹150 OFF' would generate a higher click-through rate than one saying 'BUY 2, GET A LOFAH FREE'.

nodes.label: packaging-agnostic-unless-natural-aesthetic
nodes.description: Considers packaging largely irrelevant to the purchase decision, expressing explicit indifference, with a slight, passive preference for a 'natural' look if all else is equal.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] If you could design your PERFECT body wash from scratch, describe it. Think about fragrance, texture, benefit, packaging, price — anything. A: ...Packaging: don't care.
[REAL] What do you prefer packaging that looks: A: Natural
[SYNTHETIC] Buy the basic plastic bottle at ₹249; packaging doesn’t justify ₹150 premium if ingredients and fragrance are identical.
nodes.predictive_value: A product concept focusing on 'luxury minimalist packaging' would not resonate. However, using imagery of bamboo, leaves, or earth tones might slightly enhance appeal if the product meets all other criteria.

nodes.label: browse-online-triggered-by-empty-bottle
nodes.description: The primary trigger for starting a new body wash purchase journey is the physical act of the current bottle running out, at which point they initiate online browsing.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] When your current body wash/soap runs out, what is your typical next step? A: Browse_online
[REAL] Describe the last time you tried a completely new body wash brand. What triggered the switch? Were you satisfied? A: Last tried a new brand because my old one was out of stock. Was satisfied.
nodes.predictive_value: Retargeting ads for body wash are most effective when served shortly after they've made an online search for 'body wash' or visited a personal care category page, indicating a replenishment need.

nodes.label: friend-recommendation-triggers-research-not-purchase
nodes.description: Treats recommendations from friends as a useful starting point for independent research, not as a direct purchase trigger, and values the thoroughness of the recommender's process.
evidence_quotes: [BRANCH] Rank... 5) Seeing it in my best friend's bathroom – personal but less influential unless backed by a strong endorsement.
[SYNTHETIC] Friend B — I trust their thoroughness and knowledge.
[SYNTHETIC] Look it up on Amazon/Nykaa and read reviews from strangers too.
nodes.predictive_value: If a friend raves about a body wash, this person would ask for details, then go online to read dozens of reviews before considering a purchase, rather than buying it on the spot.

nodes.label: subscription-averse-preference-for-control
nodes.description: Actively avoids subscription models for body wash, perceiving them as a loss of flexibility and control, and prefers one-time purchases even at a slightly higher cost.
evidence_quotes: [SYNTHETIC] Choose ₹349 one-time purchase; subscription adds commitment I prefer to avoid.
[SYNTHETIC] Don’t subscribe — I like the freedom to switch brands anytime.
[SYNTHETIC] Switch to a different brand that allows free purchase without subscription to keep flexibility.
nodes.predictive_value: A 'Subscribe & Save' option on a product page would be ignored; a one-time purchase button with a small 'first-order' discount would be far more effective in converting them.

nodes.label: cross-platform-price-comparison-habit
nodes.description: Routinely checks prices across Amazon, Nykaa, and quick commerce apps before purchase, valuing savings over absolute convenience.
evidence_quotes: [SYNTHETIC] Drive to DMart to save ₹50.
[SYNTHETIC] 7 min comparing prices across platforms.
[SYNTHETIC] Buy from kirana store for convenience if price is same.
nodes.predictive_value: If a brand offers a direct-to-consumer website price matching Amazon, this person would still check Amazon for reviews and may buy there for the perceived security of the platform.

nodes.label: clinical-claim-skeptic-requires-proof
nodes.description: Treats clinical or scientific claims with skepticism unless backed by accessible evidence (e.g., study results, detailed ingredient explanations), and is not swayed by the claim alone.
evidence_quotes: [SYNTHETIC] I'd need to see the clinical study results first.
[SYNTHETIC] Body wash washes off — would the ingredient even work?
[SYNTHETIC] Curious but would want dermatologist validation before trusting peptides in a body wash.
nodes.predictive_value: A claim like 'Clinically proven to increase hydration by 200%' would prompt them to look for a link to the study or search for reviews mentioning tangible results, rather than convincing them immediately.

nodes.label: functional-gifter-practical-over-luxury
nodes.description: When gifting body wash, chooses practical, trusted brands in safe, presentable packaging, prioritizing the recipient's assured usability over novelty or luxury.
evidence_quotes: [SYNTHETIC] A practical combo pack from a trusted brand (Dove, Nivea) — useful and safe.
[SYNTHETIC] Buy the new brand only if it has festive packaging that looks premium to avoid family judging it cheap.
[SYNTHETIC] A supporting item alongside perfume and skincare.
nodes.predictive_value: When buying a Diwali gift, they'd select a Dove or Nivea gift set over a more expensive but unknown D2C brand, to ensure the gift is well-received and used.

nodes.label: personal-hygiene-product-boundary-setter
nodes.description: Maintains clear boundaries around personal care items, preferring not to share body wash with others (even roommates/family) and keeping personal preferences separate.
evidence_quotes: [SYNTHETIC] Decline sharing body wash; prefer to keep personal hygiene separate.
[SYNTHETIC] Buy a cheaper body wash for shared use and keep my premium one separate.
[SYNTHETIC] Buy my own separately but split other household items.
nodes.predictive_value: A marketing message around 'family-size' or 'share the love' would not resonate. Messaging focused on 'your personal skincare ritual' would be more effective.

nodes.label: established-international-brand-trust-fallback
nodes.description: In moments of uncertainty or risk (e.g., limited info, urgent need), defaults to trusted, established international brands (Dove, Nivea) as a safe choice.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] What body wash brand(s) do you currently use? List all. A: Nivea, Pears, Lux
[SYNTHETIC] Pick Brand A for the known brand and trust despite higher price; better ingredients alone don’t outweigh brand reliability.
[SYNTHETIC] Regular Dove body wash at ₹299.
[SYNTHETIC] An established international brand (Dove, Nivea, The Body Shop).
nodes.predictive_value: If browsing a limited quick-commerce selection without time to research, they'd choose a familiar Dove variant over a cheaper or newer unknown brand.

nodes.label: P10_T020
nodes.identity_summary: A strategic, self-aware student in Ahmedabad who is a low-loyalty, pragmatic explorer of personal care. They prioritize fragrance they personally love (especially oceanic scents) and moisturizing benefits for dry skin, while being highly price-conscious within a ₹200-350 range. Their purchase decisions are dominated by online reviews and comparative research, but they remain skeptical of social media influence and are willing to switch brands for better smell, price, or to solve skin problems.

nodes.label: low-loyalty-pragmatic-switcher
nodes.description: Has low loyalty to personal care brands and is open to switching when a better option presents itself, driven by smell, skin problems, or influencer suggestions.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] How loyal are you to personal care brands? A: Low_loyalty
[REAL] What would make you switch away from your current body wash? Pick your top 3: ['better_smell','skin_problem','influencer']

[SYNTHETIC] You're buying body wash on Zepto and notice your usual brand now shows 'Price increased: was ₹249, now ₹299.'... What's your most likely reaction? A: Switch to ₹249 alternative without much thought.
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a new body wash with a compelling fragrance or a clear skin benefit at a competitive price, this person would likely switch from their current brand without hesitation.

nodes.label: oceanic-fragrance-obsessive
nodes.description: Has a strong, specific preference for oceanic/aquatic scents and rates fragrance as the #1 factor when buying personal care.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] When buying any personal care product, rank these factors from most to least important: A: ['fragrance','price','ingredients','reviews','packaging','brand','recommendation','expert']
[REAL] What fragrance families do you naturally gravitate toward? Pick your top 2: A: Ocean_aquatic,woody_musky
[REAL] If you could design your PERFECT body wash from scratch, describe it. A: Fragrance: ocean.
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a body wash concept or ad featuring a fresh, oceanic/aquatic scent description, this person would be significantly more likely to stop and consider it.

nodes.label: price-anchored-200-350-inr
nodes.description: Anchors body wash purchases to a specific price range of ₹200-350 and uses this as a primary filter when comparing options.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] How much do you typically spend on a single body wash purchase? A: 200_350
[SYNTHETIC] Imagine you're choosing between these three body washes at a store. Rank them... A: Rank: B) ₹299 mid-tier, A) ₹149 basic, C) ₹499 organic D2C.
[SYNTHETIC] Allocate 100 points across these five factors... A: Price/deal: 40, Past experience: 30...
nodes.predictive_value: When evaluating a new product, if the price falls outside the ₹200-350 range, they will require strong justification (e.g., superior size, proven clinical benefits) to consider it.

nodes.label: platform-review-dependent-decision-maker
nodes.description: Heavily relies on the review count and star ratings on the purchase platform (e.g., Amazon, Nykaa) itself as the primary source of truth, trusting volume over single expert opinions.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] When shopping online for personal care, what do you look at first? Pick your top 3: A: ['review_count','price','star_rating']
[REAL] Are there specific review platforms or trusted sources you rely on for these ratings when deciding on a new product? A: Relies on ratings from the purchase platform itself

[SYNTHETIC] You're comparing body washes online and see these ratings: Brand A: 4.2 stars, 15,000 reviews... Brand B: 4.8 stars, 120 reviews... Which do you trust more and buy? A: Brand A with 4.2 stars and 15,000 reviews.
nodes.predictive_value: A product page with high review counts (even with a slightly lower average rating) will inspire more confidence and likelihood of purchase than one with a perfect rating but few reviews.

nodes.label: skeptical-of-social-media-paid-promotion
nodes.description: Actively distrusts social media influencers and product recommendations on platforms like Instagram, citing 'paid reviews' as the main reason for hesitation.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Who do you follow or trust in the personal care space on social media? A: None
[REAL] Is there something specific about social media platforms that makes you hesitant to trust them for product recommendations? A: Paid reviews
[SYNTHETIC] Allocate 10 'trust points' across these sources... A: Amazon India reviews: 4, Mother's recommendation: 3, Reddit thread: 2, YouTube creator: 1, Dermatologist Instagram: 0.
nodes.predictive_value: An influencer-led ad campaign on Instagram or YouTube will have minimal direct impact on their purchase decision unless paired with strong platform reviews or personal recommendations.

nodes.label: strategic-researcher-before-purchase
nodes.description: Engages in deliberate, multi-source research before buying, prioritizing text reviews and ingredient lists over video content or brand marketing.
evidence_quotes: [BRANCH] When researching a new body wash online before purchase... I might glance at images but quickly read reviews and descriptions to check for skin benefits and price.
[SYNTHETIC] Imagine you have 30 minutes before buying a body wash online. How would you split that time? A: 15 min reading text reviews, 8 min comparing prices, 5 min reading ingredient lists...
[SYNTHETIC] If you had to pick ONE feature for a 'perfect' body wash product page online... A: Complete ingredient list with explanations in simple language.
nodes.predictive_value: They will spend significant time on a product page reading reviews and ingredients; a detailed 'Ingredients Explained' section would significantly increase engagement and trust.

nodes.label: personal-fragrance-over-others-approval
nodes.description: Chooses scents based on personal enjoyment rather than what they think others will find appealing, though they care about how they smell to others.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] When you're getting ready for the day, do you find yourself more focused on choosing a scent that you love personally, or one that you think others will find appealing? A: One that I love
[REAL] How much do you care about how you smell to others throughout the day? Is grooming more about feeling good yourself, or about how others perceive you, or both? A: Both
nodes.predictive_value: Advertising that focuses on 'a scent you'll love' or personal indulgence will resonate more than messaging centered on being attractive or appealing to others.

nodes.label: quick-commerce-substitute-acceptor
nodes.description: Uses quick commerce (Blinkit/Zepto) for urgent personal care needs and is willing to accept a 'close substitute' from the limited selection when necessary.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Do you use quick commerce (Blinkit, Zepto, Swiggy Instamart) for personal care? If yes, does the limited selection change what you buy? A: Yes; would buy a close substitute from limited selection

[SYNTHETIC] You're in a rush and ordering body wash on Zepto/Blinkit for 10-minute delivery. Your usual brand isn't available... How do you choose? A: Pick the one closest to your usual brand's description.
nodes.predictive_value: In urgent, low-inventory scenarios (e.g., quick commerce), they can be captured by competitors if their preferred brand is out of stock, especially if the alternative matches key attributes like texture or benefit.

nodes.label: india-specific-claim-responsive
nodes.description: Highly responsive to marketing claims that a product is formulated for 'Indian skin types,' 'hard water conditions,' or uses 'India-specific' ingredients or recipes.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] What claims or product descriptions on a body wash would make you stop and read more? Pick top 3: ['india_specific','derm_recommended','ph_balanced']
[REAL] Would you be interested in a body wash specifically formulated for Indian skin types and hard water conditions? A: {'rating':5,'explanation':''}

[SYNTHETIC] You see a body wash on Amazon India with the description: 'Made with an ancient tribal recipe from Northeast India'... Would you try it... A: I would try it; primary driver is the India-specific tribal recipe claim.
nodes.predictive_value: Product concepts or ads that lead with 'Made for Indian skin' or 'Fights hard water dryness' will immediately capture their attention and drive deeper research.

nodes.label: premium-for-proven-efficacy-only
nodes.description: Willing to pay a 10-20% premium only for attributes with tangible, proven benefits like 'clinical-grade, lab-tested formulation' or 'Made in India,' but not for sustainability or natural claims alone.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] What is the maximum premium (%) you would pay for a product that aligns with your values? ... {'Clinical-grade, lab-tested formulation':'10_20','Made in India':'10_20','Sustainable/eco-friendly packaging':'0','Ayurvedic or traditional ingredients':'0'}

[SYNTHETIC] You see a body wash at ₹349 that claims '100% recyclable packaging' and donates ₹10 per bottle to ocean cleanup... How much of the ₹80 difference do you feel is justified? A: ₹0 — these claims don't influence what I'm willing to pay.
nodes.predictive_value: They will reject premium pricing based on vague 'natural' or 'eco-friendly' claims but may accept it for products with clinical testing certifications or strong 'Made in India' value propositions.

nodes.label: mood-dependent-grooming-ritualist
nodes.description: Their relationship with bathing and grooming is mood-dependent, making them more receptive to marketing for self-care or stress relief during low moods.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] How would you describe your relationship with bathing/grooming? A: Mood_dependent
[BRANCH] After a stressful day, I'm much more likely to click on that ad and consider buying the body wash for instant relief—it feels like a necessary self-care treat.
nodes.predictive_value: During periods of stress or low mood, they are disproportionately more likely to click on and purchase body washes marketed as calming, relaxing, or self-care treats.

nodes.label: thick-granular-texture-idealizer
nodes.description: Has a specific, non-negotiable preference for a thick, creamy body wash with a slightly granular texture that provides a cooling sensation and leaves no stickiness.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] What texture do you prefer in a body wash? A: Thick_cream
[REAL] If you could design your PERFECT body wash from scratch, describe it. ... Texture: slightly granular, thick liquid.

[REAL] Is there anything about the texture or how it feels on your skin that you wish could be improved? A: ... granular texture in the liquid, cooling sensation during use, no stickiness left on skin after wash
nodes.predictive_value: Product demos or descriptions that emphasize a 'thick, creamy lather with gentle exfoliating granules' and a 'non-sticky, clean rinse' will strongly align with their ideal and drive purchase intent.

nodes.label: self-aware-impulse-purchase-restrainer
nodes.description: Exhibits high self-awareness to avoid unnecessary purchases, letting coupons expire and refusing to stock up prematurely even on good deals.
evidence_quotes: [BRANCH] I'd let the coupon expire. My current body wash works fine, and I prefer to stick with what I know until I actually run out. I'm self-aware about avoiding unnecessary purchases...

[SYNTHETIC] You're browsing Nykaa and find your regular body wash at ₹349. There's a combo offer: buy 2 for ₹549... You only need one right now. What do you do? A: Buy just one at ₹349. I don't need extra stock now and prefer to avoid unnecessary purchases.
nodes.predictive_value: Flash sales and limited-time coupons will be less effective unless they coincide precisely with their need state (i.e., running out).

nodes.label: trust-personal-recommendation-over-expert
nodes.description: Places higher trust in recommendations from friends and family ('seeing it in a friend's bathroom') than in expert or dermatologist endorsements presented in ads.
evidence_quotes: [BRANCH] Rank these five purchase triggers from most to least likely... I'd go: 1) Seeing it in my best friend's bathroom – personal connections make me curious and trusting. ... 5) Dermatologist's recommendation – feels too clinical...
[SYNTHETIC] Allocate 10 'trust points' across these sources... A: Amazon India reviews: 4, Mother's recommendation: 3...
nodes.predictive_value: A marketing campaign leveraging 'word-of-mouth' or 'friend-tested' social proof will be more effective than one featuring a dermatologist in a lab coat.

nodes.label: ad-receptive-to-luxury-spa-fantasy
nodes.description: Prefers advertising that portrays body wash as a luxurious, spa-like self-care ritual rather than a mundane, everyday utility.
evidence_quotes: [BRANCH] Two body wash ads play on TV... I'd prefer Ad 1 with the spa setting. It makes the body wash feel like a premium treat for those times I want to pamper myself...
[REAL] How would you describe your personality in 3-5 words? A: Strategic. Intense. Self-aware
nodes.predictive_value: Visual ads featuring serene, premium settings (spas, luxury bathrooms) will be more effective at capturing their interest and creating perceived value than ads showing hurried morning routines.

nodes.label: ingredient-list-scanner-for-key-red-flags
nodes.description: Actively scans ingredient lists, but primarily to recognize and verify a few key claims (like 'Sulfate-Free' or 'Paraben-Free') rather than conducting deep chemical analysis.
evidence_quotes: [SYNTHETIC] What if a body wash listed 'Aqua, Sodium Laureth Sulfate, Cocamidopropyl Betaine...' on the back label? Would you... A: Recognize a few key ones like SLS.
[SYNTHETIC] You notice your body wash has 'Paraben-Free' printed on it. What would you most likely do? A: Flip the bottle to verify in the ingredient list.
nodes.predictive_value: Product packaging that highlights key free-from claims (Sulfate-Free, Paraben-Free) prominently on the front label will satisfy their initial scrutiny and reduce barrier to purchase.

nodes.label: ayurvedic-openness-if-price-competitive
nodes.description: Open to trying Ayurvedic or traditional products, but only if they are priced competitively (at or below their usual range) and not at a 'wellness premium.'
evidence_quotes: [SYNTHETIC] You see an Instagram ad for a new body wash with 4.5-star ratings and 10,000+ reviews at ₹399. The same day, your mother suggests trying a ₹120 Ayurvedic body wash from Patanjali... Which do you buy? A: Buy the Patanjali Ayurvedic body wash at ₹120.
[REAL] What is the maximum premium (%) you would pay for a product that aligns with your values? ... 'Ayurvedic or traditional ingredients':'0'
nodes.predictive_value: An Ayurvedic body wash priced at ₹199-249 with familiar ingredient claims (neem, tulsi) will be considered, but one priced at ₹499 for 'ancient wisdom' will be rejected.

nodes.label: deal-preference-cash-discount-over-bundles
nodes.description: Prefers straightforward cash discounts (e.g., '₹100 off') over complex bundling ('Buy 2 Get 1 Free') or future-value offers like cashback.
evidence_quotes: [SYNTHETIC] Imagine your body wash costs ₹299. Rank these discount presentations from most to least appealing: A: '₹100 off — now ₹199', B: '33% off', C: 'Buy 2 Get 1 Free'... A: A) ₹100 off — now ₹199, B) Free shipping + ₹50 off, C) Buy 2 Get 1 Free...

[SYNTHETIC] Two body wash brands you equally like are running Diwali offers... Brand A offers buy-2-get-1-free. Brand B offers a ₹100 cashback on a single bottle... Which offer do you pick? A: Pick ₹100 cashback on single bottle since I only need one now.
nodes.predictive_value: Promotions should lead with simple, immediate price reduction messages ('Flat ₹100 OFF') to maximize conversion, rather than multi-buy or cashback mechanics.

nodes.label: dermatologist-claim-skeptic-requiring-proof
nodes.description: While 'dermatologist-recommended' is a stopping claim, they remain skeptical and require seeing clinical study results or third-party validation beyond the brand's own statement.
evidence_quotes: [SYNTHETIC] What if your regular ₹299 body wash launched a 'dermatologist-recommended' version at ₹399 with the same ingredients but clinical testing certification? Would you upgrade? A: C) I'd need to see the clinical study results first.

[SYNTHETIC] You're browsing body wash on Amazon India and notice a product has a 'Clinically Tested' badge, but... no link to the actual clinical study... another... has a detailed blog post... Which product do you add to cart? A: Add the one with detailed blog post explaining ingredients.
nodes.predictive_value: A 'Dermatologist Tested' claim will grab attention, but to convince them to pay more, the ad or product page must link to or summarize the actual test methodology and results.

nodes.label: friend-recommendation-triggers-research-not-blind-buy
nodes.description: A friend's recommendation triggers curiosity and initial interest, but is followed by independent online research (checking reviews, price) before purchase, not an immediate buy.
evidence_quotes: [SYNTHETIC] A WhatsApp group of your college friends is buzzing about a new body wash brand... What's your most likely action? A: Look it up on Amazon/Nykaa and read reviews from strangers too.
[SYNTHETIC] Two friends independently recommend two different body wash brands to you... Whose recommendation carries more weight? A: I'd look up both brands online before deciding.
nodes.predictive_value: A referral program that incentivizes friends to share will generate initial interest, but the product must then have strong, verifiable online reviews to convert that interest into a sale.

nodes.label: travel-size-logical-for-contextual-need
nodes.description: Sees travel-size or sachet formats as logical solutions for specific, temporary needs like traveling or trying a new variant, not for regular use.
evidence_quotes: [SYNTHETIC] You discover that your ₹299 body wash is available as a ₹30 single-use sachet at your local kirana store. Under what circumstance would you buy the sachet instead? A: Buy sachet when traveling and need something small.

[SYNTHETIC] You're shopping for body wash on Zepto and notice your regular body wash comes in two sizes... a 100ml travel size at ₹129. You only need it for the next two weeks. Which size do you pick? A: Pick the 100ml travel size for two weeks.
nodes.predictive_value: They will be receptive to travel-sized offerings or trial kits positioned for 'your weekend getaway' or 'try before you commit,' but will default to full-size for regular replenishment.

nodes.label: browsing-initiates-at-run-out-moment
nodes.description: The trigger for a new body wash purchase is typically when the current one runs out, at which point they begin actively browsing options online.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] When your current body wash/soap runs out, what is your typical next step? A: Browse_online
[BRANCH] ...I prefer to stick with what I know until I actually run out.
nodes.predictive_value: Retargeting ads or 'replenishment reminder' notifications are most effective when timed to when their current product is likely finishing (e.g., 2-3 weeks after last purchase).

nodes.label: prefers-physical-test-for-texture-scent
nodes.description: Values the ability to physically test a product's texture and smell in-store, considering it a key part of the decision process when available.
evidence_quotes: [BRANCH] In-store, I look at the bottle and feel if it's thick and granular like I want.

[SYNTHETIC] Imagine two scenarios for trying a new body wash. Scenario A: You visit a pop-up store where you can smell, feel, and test... Scenario B: You get a 75% discount online but can't test it. Which scenario makes you more likely to buy? A: Scenario A: Pop-up store with testing before buying.
nodes.predictive_value: In-store sampling stations or 'testers available' call-outs will significantly increase their likelihood of purchasing a new brand in physical retail environments.

nodes.label: value-size-acceptor-for-clear-per-ml-saving
nodes.description: Willing to buy a larger size (e.g., 500ml) if the per-ml price is clearly cheaper, indicating a focus on long-term value when the upfront cost is justified.
evidence_quotes: [SYNTHETIC] What if your favorite body wash suddenly became available only in a 500ml size at ₹549 (previously 250ml at ₹299)? The per-ml price is slightly cheaper. What would you do? A: Buy the larger 500ml size at ₹549 for better value per ml.
[REAL] What is the most you have ever paid for a body wash? What was it and was it worth it? A: Highest paid: ~_300 for a large pack. Worth it due to price-to-size ratio and smell.
nodes.predictive_value: Product pages that clearly display 'value per 100ml' or '30% more free' will be effective in upselling them to a larger size.

nodes.label: brand-perception-driven-by-formula-not-ownership
nodes.description: Their perception of a brand is tied to the specific product formulation and experience; discovering it's made in the same factory as a cheaper brand may cause momentary irritation but won't change their loyalty if the product itself is good.
evidence_quotes: [SYNTHETIC] You discover that a body wash you've been buying at ₹299 from a 'premium natural' brand is actually manufactured by the same factory that makes a ₹129 body wash for a mass brand. Would this information... A: B) Make me feel cheated but I'd probably still buy the ₹299 one for its branding/fragrance.

[SYNTHETIC] Imagine your body wash brand gets acquired by a large conglomerate... The product formula stays the same. Does your perception change? A: B) No, as long as the product is the same, I don't care who owns it.
nodes.predictive_value: Negative PR about a brand's parent company or manufacturing practices will have limited impact if the specific product continues to deliver on fragrance and skin feel.

links.dimension: Visual versus textual information processing preference

links.position: Neutral to ad style, high on product benefits

links.dimension: Impulse versus deliberate purchase decision speed

links.mechanism: Their strategic approach to purchases reinforces the establishment of a strict price anchor; they use the ₹200-₹350 range as a key parameter in their value maximization calculus, rejecting options that fall outside it without overwhelming justification.

The primacy of personal fragrance enjoyment ('one that I love') is the driving force behind their specific scent preferences (oceanic, musky); the social component ('both') is a secondary consideration that does not override their personal taste.

Distrust of curated, often paid social media content leads them to seek validation from the perceived 'wisdom of the crowd' on e-commerce platforms, where they believe reviews are more authentic and representative of real user experience.

Their trust in clinical/dermatologist authority causes them to focus on specific, scientifically-backed ingredient claims (like pH-balanced, sulfate-free) rather than vague 'natural' marketing, leading to label verification behavior.

Willingness to experiment is not reckless; it is enabled and governed by a prerequisite of thorough research (reviews, trials) which mitigates the perceived risk of switching away from a known, satisfactory product.

Their planned, need-based shopping means they evaluate deals at the moment of actual purchase. Complex bundles (BOGO) often require buying more than needed, contradicting their 'buy just one' philosophy, so they prefer simple discounts that apply to the single unit they intend to purchase.

Self-awareness of mood influence allows them to cognitively separate transient emotional states from their planned, rational purchase cycle. They acknowledge mood might trigger interest, but it doesn't override the rule of waiting until they run out.

Using quick commerce specifically for urgent replenishment causes high tolerance for substitution. The channel's constraint (limited selection) and the context (immediate need) override their usual preference for researched choices, making them accept a 'good enough' alternative.

Their core trait of maximizing value for money directly contradicts paying any premium for sustainability attributes, which they assign zero monetary value. This creates a tension where eco-friendly products must compete on equal price and performance terms to be considered.

links.dimension: Visual versus textual information processing preference
Impulse versus deliberate purchase decision speed

links.mechanism: Because fragrance is their primary decision criterion, they have developed specific, strong preferences for certain scent families (ocean, woody/musky) that guide their choices.
Their careful balancing of price against tangible benefits causes them to reject sustainability premiums, which they perceive as not delivering direct personal value.
Their price-value calculus leads them to prefer simple, transparent discounts that allow for easy comparison, avoiding complex schemes that obscure the actual saving.
Their reliance on platform reviews (perceived as more authentic) is reinforced by their active distrust of social media recommendations, which they view as biased and paid.
Their trust in real-world peer experiences translates to a preference for advertising that mirrors those authentic, relatable scenarios rather than manufactured aspirational fantasies.
Their low loyalty creates an inherent openness to switching, which manifests as deliberate browsing at each purchase cycle rather than auto-replenishment.

Their strategic, deliberate browsing pattern conflicts with their mood-driven receptivity to certain appeals (like stress relief), creating windows where emotional triggers might override their usual calculated approach.

While skeptical of marketing claims about ingredients, they paradoxically rely heavily on crowd-sourced platform reviews (which can be equally unreliable) rather than seeking authoritative expert sources, creating a tension in their information verification process.

Their specific skin concerns make them receptive to paying more for formulations with clinically proven benefits that address those concerns, creating a logical link between problem and solution investment.

Their belief in Indian brands offering better value makes them particularly receptive to products specifically formulated for Indian conditions, seeing them as more relevant and potentially better performing.

links.dimension: Visual versus textual information processing preference

links.mechanism: Because scent is so paramount and subjective, the desire to physically experience the fragrance firsthand reinforces their preference for trying samples before buying a full bottle.
Their constant calculation of price-value across options makes them unwilling to stick with a brand out of loyalty, especially if a competitor offers a better deal or more appealing feature set.

While they deeply trust friends, they are skeptical of broader social proof from influencers. This tension is resolved by prioritizing aggregated anonymous reviews (seen as more authentic) over curated influencer endorsements.

Their willingness to pay more only for clinically proven or India-specific benefits directs their ingredient scrutiny towards verifying those specific, performance-oriented claims rather than general 'clean beauty' labels.
The limited selection on quick commerce platforms during urgent replenishment moments forces or encourages brand switching, reinforcing their low-loyalty pattern.

Their variable grooming moods make the sensory and emotional lift provided by a beloved fragrance an even more important constant, as it can enhance both a pampering or an energizing shower experience.
Their strategic nature drives them to seek external validation and reduce risk, making aggregated user reviews and ratings a critical, efficient data source for decision-making.
